does: This paper proposes a hybrid reward modeling framework for aligning multimodal large language models that combines model-based rewards (learned from synthetic and human feedback) with rule-based rewards (domain-specific heuristics providing explicit correctness signals). The approach extends beyond single accuracy scores by incorporating multi-aspect rewards that enforce instruction adherence and a generalized length-penalty reward to stabilize training. The framework optimizes MLLM policies through reinforcement learning, demonstrating consistent improvements across multiple multimodal benchmarks compared to monolithic reward approaches.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature in MLLM alignment and reward modeling for language models, extending recent advances in preference-based training and reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF). It responds to limitations of single-signal reward models that have dominated recent MLLM tuning approaches, connecting to earlier work on multi-objective reward design and confidence calibration in learned reward models. The hybrid approach combines insights from rule-based evaluation methods used in NLP with learned reward models, synthesizing two parallel traditions in model alignment while addressing the challenge of capturing diverse aspects of human preferences in multimodal settings.
enables: This framework opens paths toward more robust MLLM alignment by providing a flexible architecture for incorporating domain-specific knowledge alongside learned signals. It enables future work on automatically discovering complementary reward aspects, designing better rule-based heuristics for different modalities, and calibrating confidence across heterogeneous reward signals. The multi-aspect formulation supports downstream applications requiring specific behavioral properties beyond accuracy, such as following format instructions or reasoning transparency. The approach also provides a foundation for exploring how to weight and balance competing reward signals, potentially advancing research into interpretable and controllable MLLM behavior.
